A lake's nutrient cycles operate within the confines of the water body and its immediate watershed. A tropical forest's energy flows are contained within the extent of the canopy and the soil beneath it. A coral reef's matter cycles are largely internal to the reef structure. These cases invite the generalisation that an ecosystem consists of an interacting biotic community and its physical environment in which energy and nutrients cycle within spatial boundaries.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the ecology of long-distance avian migration. Consider the migratory system that connects Arctic breeding grounds with tropical wintering areas through which millions of shorebirds travel annually. The birds form an interacting community: they compete for invertebrate prey at coastal staging sites, transport vast quantities of nutrients in the form of guano from one region to another, and serve as prey for raptors and other predators at each stop along their route. Energy and nutrients cycle through this system as the birds consume resources in one hemisphere and deposit them in another, linking the food webs of the Arctic tundra, the temperate coastline, and the tropical mudflat into a single functional network. Yet this system has no spatial boundaries within which the principal flows occur; the flows extend across thousands of miles and connect regions on different continents. The third condition of the proposed definition is not met. Nevertheless, the migratory network functions as an integrated ecological system whose disruption at any point — the loss of a critical staging site, the degradation of the Arctic breeding grounds — reverberates through the whole. The background cases, it appears, sampled only spatially compact ecosystems, and the generalisation from them mistook spatial boundedness for a necessary feature of ecological integration.

background_cases: The red fox and the arctic fox are classified as distinct species because, although they share a recent common ancestor, they cannot interbreed to produce fertile offspring. The reproductive barrier between them is intrinsic: their gametes are incompatible regardless of the environment in which they encounter one another.

The horse and the donkey are classified as distinct species because, although they can mate and produce offspring, the offspring — mules — are sterile. The inability of mules to reproduce establishes a reproductive barrier that prevents gene flow between horses and donkeys.

The eastern and western meadowlarks are classified as distinct species despite nearly identical plumage because they do not interbreed in the wild. Their songs differ sufficiently that females of each type refuse to mate with males of the other, establishing a behavioural reproductive barrier.
definition: Two populations constitute distinct species if and only if (i) they do not interbreed in nature, (ii) when hybridisation does occur, the offspring are infertile or inviable, and (iii) there exists an identifiable mechanism — whether genetic, morphological, or behavioural — that maintains the reproductive barrier.
conditions: The two populations do not interbreed in nature
When hybridisation occurs the offspring are infertile or inviable
There exists an identifiable mechanism maintaining the reproductive barrier
counterexample: Suppose two populations of oak trees grow in adjacent regions and do not normally interbreed, satisfying condition (i). When hybridisation does occur through deliberate cross-pollination, the offspring are infertile, satisfying condition (ii). And the reproductive barrier is maintained by a difference in flowering time, satisfying condition (iii). All three conditions are met. Yet the difference in flowering time is entirely a product of the different climates in the two regions. If seedlings of one population are transplanted to the other region, they adjust their flowering time within a single generation and interbreed freely with the local population, producing fully fertile offspring. The barrier is not intrinsic to the populations but is an artefact of environmental circumstance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the reproductive barriers are intrinsic to the biology of the organisms, persisting regardless of environmental manipulation. The definition, generalised from these cases, fails to require that the barrier be intrinsic rather than environmentally contingent. An environmentally induced barrier that disappears upon transplantation satisfies the definition whilst failing to establish the kind of permanent reproductive isolation that genuine species status requires.
passage:
The classification of organisms into distinct species typically rests upon the observation of reproductive barriers that prevent gene flow between populations. The red fox and the arctic fox cannot produce fertile hybrids, regardless of environment. The horse and the donkey produce only sterile mules. The eastern and western meadowlarks refuse to interbreed because of differences in song. These cases invite a general characterisation: two populations are distinct species when they do not interbreed in nature, any hybrids are infertile or inviable, and an identifiable mechanism maintains the barrier.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the study of oaks. Suppose two populations of oak trees in adjacent regions do not normally interbreed. When cross-pollination is artificially induced, the offspring prove infertile. And the barrier is maintained by a difference in flowering time between the two regions. All three conditions are met. Yet the flowering-time difference is entirely a product of the differing climates: seedlings transplanted from one region to the other adjust their flowering schedule within a single generation and interbreed freely with the local population, producing fully fertile offspring. The reproductive barrier is not intrinsic to the biology of the trees but is an artefact of environmental circumstance. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the mechanism maintaining the barrier be intrinsic to the organisms, persisting regardless of environmental manipulation, so that the barrier reflects genuine biological divergence rather than a contingent environmental separation that transplantation can dissolve.

background_cases: When a previously healthy man develops tuberculosis after inhaling Mycobacterium tuberculosis, we identify the bacterium as the cause of his disease. The pathogen invades the lungs, provokes an immune response, and produces the characteristic symptoms of cough, fever, and weight loss. The causal chain from pathogen to symptoms is direct and well understood.

When a woman develops malaria after being bitten by an Anopheles mosquito carrying Plasmodium falciparum, the parasite is identified as the cause of her illness. The parasite invades her red blood cells, multiplies, and produces the cyclical fevers and anaemia characteristic of the disease. The causal chain is again direct and traceable.

When a child contracts measles after exposure to the measles virus, the virus is identified as the cause. It infects the respiratory tract, spreads through the bloodstream, and produces the characteristic rash and fever. The causal path from viral invasion to symptoms is well established.
definition: A condition D is a disease caused by pathogen P if and only if (i) P is present in the organism exhibiting D, (ii) the symptoms of D arise through a causal chain initiated by P's invasion of the organism, and (iii) removal or suppression of P leads to the resolution of D's symptoms.
conditions: P is present in the organism exhibiting D
The symptoms of D arise through a causal chain initiated by P's invasion
Removal or suppression of P leads to resolution of symptoms
counterexample: Suppose a man is infected with Helicobacter pylori. The bacterium is present in his stomach, satisfying condition (i). He develops chronic stomach inflammation, and the inflammatory symptoms arise through a causal chain initiated by the bacterium's colonisation of the gastric lining, satisfying condition (ii). And when the bacterium is eradicated by antibiotics, the acute inflammation resolves, satisfying condition (iii). All three conditions are met. Yet years after the infection was cleared, the man develops stomach cancer in the tissue that was chronically inflamed. The cancer was set in motion by the bacterium's prolonged presence, but it continues to progress long after the bacterium has been removed. The disease — the cancer — does not resolve upon the suppression of the pathogen, because the pathogenic process initiated a self-sustaining pathological cascade.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the diseases they describe are sustained by the ongoing presence of the pathogen, so that removing the pathogen halts the disease process. The definition, generalised from these cases, fails to accommodate diseases in which the pathogen initiates a self-sustaining pathological process that persists independently of the pathogen's continued presence. In such cases, the causal chain runs from pathogen to disease but the disease acquires its own momentum.
passage:
The identification of infectious disease typically follows a straightforward causal pattern: a pathogen invades the organism, initiates a chain of physiological disruption, and produces characteristic symptoms that resolve when the pathogen is suppressed. Tuberculosis, malaria, and measles all exemplify this structure. These cases invite a general characterisation: a disease is caused by a pathogen when the pathogen is present, the symptoms arise through a causal chain it initiates, and the symptoms resolve upon the pathogen's removal.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the study of Helicobacter pylori and gastric cancer. Suppose a man is infected with H. pylori. The bacterium is present in his stomach; the chronic inflammation arises through a causal chain initiated by the bacterium's colonisation; and when antibiotics eradicate the bacterium, the acute inflammation resolves. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet years after the infection has been cleared, the man develops stomach cancer in the tissue that was chronically inflamed. The cancer was set in motion by the bacterium's prolonged presence, but it continues to progress long after the pathogen has been removed, because the chronic inflammation initiated genetic changes that sustain the malignancy independently. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to accommodate, is that the pathogen's causal contribution was ongoing: the disease process depended at every stage upon the pathogen's continued presence. In the gastric cancer case, the pathogen initiates a self-sustaining pathological cascade that no longer requires the pathogen for its perpetuation, exposing a limit of the inductively generalised definition.

background_cases: The long neck of the giraffe is widely regarded as an adaptation for reaching high foliage. Ancestral giraffes with slightly longer necks gained a feeding advantage, survived and reproduced at higher rates, and over many generations natural selection increased the average neck length in the population. The trait enhances fitness, was shaped by selection, and serves the function for which it was selected.

The camouflage colouration of the peppered moth is regarded as an adaptation for avoiding predation. Moths whose colouration matched the colour of tree bark were less likely to be eaten by birds, and natural selection favoured darker moths as industrial pollution darkened the bark. The trait enhances fitness, was shaped by selection, and serves the function of predator evasion.

The hollow bones of birds are regarded as an adaptation for flight. Ancestral birds with lighter skeletons enjoyed improved aerodynamic performance, and natural selection over many generations produced the characteristically hollow bone structure. The trait enhances fitness, was shaped by selection, and serves the function of facilitating flight.
definition: A trait T in a population is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances the fitness of organisms possessing it in their current environment, (ii) T was shaped by natural selection operating on heritable variation over many generations, and (iii) T currently serves the function for which it was originally selected.
conditions: T enhances the fitness of organisms possessing it in their current environment
T was shaped by natural selection operating on heritable variation over many generations
T currently serves the function for which it was originally selected
counterexample: Suppose a species of beetle possesses an unusually thick exoskeleton. The thick exoskeleton enhances the beetle's fitness in its current desert environment by reducing water loss. The trait was shaped by natural selection over many generations. And the trait currently serves the function for which it was originally selected, namely protection against desiccation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet in the beetle's current environment, the thick exoskeleton also serves a second, equally important function: it protects the beetle from a recently arrived predator that crushes thin-shelled insects. This second function was never selected for; it is a fortuitous by-product of a trait selected for desiccation resistance. The definition, by requiring that the trait serve only the function for which it was selected, cannot account for this additional adaptive significance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each trait serves a single identifiable function that coincides with its selective history. The definition, generalised from these cases, fails to accommodate traits whose selective history endowed them with properties that serve additional, unselected functions in the current environment. A trait may be an adaptation in its current setting for reasons that extend beyond the original selective pressure, and the definition has no room for this possibility.
passage:
The paradigmatic examples of biological adaptation share a tidy structure: the giraffe's long neck was selected for reaching high foliage and continues to serve that purpose; the peppered moth's colouration was selected for predator evasion and continues to conceal the moth; the bird's hollow bones were selected for flight and continue to facilitate it. These cases invite a general characterisation: a trait is an adaptation when it enhances fitness, was shaped by selection, and currently serves the function for which it was selected.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from desert entomology. Suppose a species of beetle possesses an unusually thick exoskeleton. The thickness enhances fitness by reducing water loss, it was shaped by natural selection over many generations, and it currently serves its original function of desiccation resistance. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet in the beetle's present environment, a recently arrived predator crushes thin-shelled insects, and the thick exoskeleton also protects the beetle from this predator. This second adaptive function was never selected for; it is a fortuitous consequence of a trait shaped by entirely different pressures. The definition, generalised from cases in which each trait serves a single function coinciding with its selective history, cannot account for this additional adaptive significance. What the background cases share is a neat alignment of selective history and current function, and the definition inherits this neatness as a requirement. But nature is under no obligation to maintain such alignment, and a trait may be genuinely adaptive in its present environment for reasons that extend well beyond its original selective rationale.

background_cases: When a ball is dropped from a height, the gravitational force between the ball and the earth causes the ball to accelerate downward. The force acts at a distance, its magnitude depends upon the masses of the two bodies and the distance between them, and its effect is to change the ball's state of motion.

When two magnets are placed near each other with like poles facing, a repulsive magnetic force pushes them apart. The force acts at a distance, its magnitude depends upon the strength of the magnets and their separation, and it changes their states of motion.

When a charged particle passes near a second charged particle, the electrostatic force between them deflects the first particle's trajectory. The force acts at a distance, its magnitude depends upon the charges and the distance, and it alters the particle's state of motion.
definition: An interaction F between two bodies constitutes a physical force if and only if (i) F acts at a distance between the two bodies without requiring material contact, (ii) the magnitude of F depends upon intrinsic properties of the bodies and the distance between them, and (iii) F produces a change in the state of motion of at least one of the bodies.
conditions: F acts at a distance between two bodies without requiring material contact
The magnitude of F depends upon intrinsic properties of the bodies and the distance between them
F produces a change in the state of motion of at least one of the bodies
counterexample: Suppose a man pushes a heavy crate across a floor. The push produces a change in the crate's state of motion. Yet the force does not act at a distance; it requires direct material contact between the man's hands and the crate. The definition, which requires action at a distance, would exclude this paradigmatic case of a physical force. If one attempts to salvage the account by including contact forces, the requirement that F act at a distance must be abandoned, and the definition loses one of its three conditions.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the forces in question happen to be fundamental forces that act at a distance. The definition, generalised from these cases, inherits the action-at-a-distance requirement and thereby excludes contact forces, which are among the most familiar and intuitively compelling examples of physical forces. The inductive base was too narrow: by drawing only upon cases of action at a distance, the generalisation failed to accommodate the broader class of interactions that includes direct physical contact.
passage:
The most celebrated forces in the history of natural philosophy share a structural feature that tempts one towards a definition that is, upon closer inspection, too narrow. Gravitational attraction between masses, magnetic repulsion between like poles, and electrostatic deflection between charges all act at a distance, depend upon intrinsic properties and separation, and produce changes in motion. These cases invite a general characterisation: a physical force is an interaction that acts at a distance, depends upon intrinsic properties and distance, and changes the state of motion of at least one body.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the most ordinary of physical interactions. Suppose a man pushes a heavy crate across a floor. The push produces a clear change in the crate's state of motion. Yet it does not act at a distance; it requires direct material contact between the man's hands and the surface of the crate. The definition, which demands action at a distance, would exclude this paradigmatic instance of a physical force. If one attempts to salvage the account by relaxing the distance requirement to include contact, condition (i) must be abandoned entirely, and the definition loses a third of its content. What the background cases share is that they are all instances of fundamental forces that happen to act at a distance. The definition, generalised from this restricted class, inherits the action-at-a-distance requirement and thereby excludes contact forces, which are among the most common and intuitively compelling interactions in the physical world. The inductive base was too narrow, and the generalisation fails where a broader range of cases would have demanded a more capacious formulation.

background_cases: A temperate forest supports a stable network of producers, herbivores, and predators, cycling nutrients and energy through well-defined trophic levels over many decades.

A coral reef in tropical waters sustains thousands of interdependent species, each fulfilling a recognisable ecological role, and the system persists in a roughly stable state from century to century.
A freshwater lake contains phytoplankton, zooplankton, fish, and decomposers, all linked by feeding relationships and biogeochemical cycles that maintain the lake's productivity year after year.
definition: A natural system constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) it comprises multiple trophic levels linked by energy transfer, (ii) it sustains biogeochemical cycling of essential elements, and (iii) it persists in a recognisable steady state over extended periods.
conditions: it comprises multiple trophic levels linked by energy transfer
it sustains biogeochemical cycling of essential elements
it persists in a recognisable steady state over extended periods
counterexample: Suppose that a wildfire sweeps through a boreal forest, reducing the established ecosystem to ash and bare mineral soil. Within weeks, bacteria and fungi colonise the burned ground; within months, grasses and herbaceous plants take hold, attracting insects and small mammals. Energy flows through nascent trophic levels and biogeochemical cycling resumes in rudimentary form. Yet the system is in rapid directional change — new species arrive each season, competitive hierarchies shift, and the community bears little resemblance to what it will become in fifty years. The third condition is plainly unsatisfied, yet ecologists would recognise this as an ecosystem undergoing primary succession.
abductive_insight: Our background cases were all drawn from mature, stable systems — forests, reefs, lakes — in which persistence was a conspicuous feature. We generalised that steady-state persistence is a necessary condition of ecosystem status. The post-fire case reveals that this generalisation reflects the stability of our chosen examples rather than a deep truth about what ecosystems are. Systems in transition possess the essential functional properties — trophic structure and nutrient cycling — without exhibiting the constancy that our mature examples displayed. The inductive inference from stable cases to a stability requirement was unwarranted.
passage:
When we survey the paradigmatic instances from which our concept of an ecosystem is drawn, a pattern emerges with considerable regularity. The temperate forest, with its layered canopy and complex food web, persists in broadly similar form across decades. The coral reef sustains its dazzling community of organisms from one century to the next. The freshwater lake cycles its nutrients through recognisable trophic levels year after year. From these cases one might reasonably infer that an ecosystem is a natural system characterised by multiple trophic levels linked by energy transfer, biogeochemical cycling of essential elements, and persistence in a recognisable steady state over extended periods. The three conditions appear jointly to distinguish ecosystems from transient aggregations of organisms and from purely abiotic systems.

Yet the confidence of this generalisation may rest upon an unexamined bias in our sample. Let us imagine a boreal forest devastated by wildfire. The established community — its trees, its mammals, its intricate food web — has been reduced to ash. Within weeks, however, microbial colonisers appear on the charred substrate. Within months, pioneer plants establish themselves, and insects and small rodents follow. Energy flows, albeit in modest quantities, through embryonic trophic levels. Nitrogen and carbon begin to cycle through the soil and the nascent vegetation. The first two conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied in at least rudimentary form. But the system is far from any steady state; it is changing rapidly and directionally, with new arrivals displacing earlier colonists and competitive relationships in continuous flux. The third condition is unmet. Nevertheless, ecologists would not hesitate to study this system as an ecosystem — a young one, to be sure, but an ecosystem all the same. We are brought to recognise that our original induction was drawn from cases that happened to be stable, and that we mistook a common feature of mature systems for a necessary feature of all systems. The post-fire landscape teaches us that trophic structure and nutrient cycling may constitute the essence of an ecosystem, while steady-state persistence is a contingent property of those that have had time to mature.

background_cases: The long neck of the giraffe enables it to browse on foliage that shorter-necked competitors cannot reach, thereby enhancing its fitness in its particular environment. The trait arose gradually through differential survival and reproduction.

The cryptic colouration of the peppered moth allows it to blend with lichen-covered tree bark, reducing predation. Moths matching the background survived at higher rates, and the trait spread through the population.
The antifreeze proteins of certain Arctic fish prevent ice crystals from forming in their blood, enabling them to survive in sub-zero waters where other species cannot.
definition: A trait is an adaptation if and only if (i) it confers a fitness advantage upon the organism in its environment, (ii) it arose through the gradual accumulation of heritable variations favoured by natural selection, and (iii) it serves a specific function that explains why it was selected.
conditions: it confers a fitness advantage upon the organism in its environment
it arose through the gradual accumulation of heritable variations favoured by natural selection
it serves a specific function that explains why it was selected
counterexample: Suppose that a species of beetle possesses a bright red pigmentation in its exoskeleton. The pigmentation arose not through gradual selection but as a pleiotropic by-product of a gene selected for its role in producing a toxic compound that deters predators. The red colour happens to serve as an aposematic signal, warning predators of the beetle's toxicity, and thus confers a fitness advantage. It serves a recognisable function. But it did not arise through the gradual accumulation of variations selected for that function — it appeared as a side-effect of selection acting on a different trait. The second condition is not satisfied in the manner the definition requires.
abductive_insight: Our background cases were all instances in which a trait's function and its selective history neatly coincided — the neck for reaching foliage, the colour for camouflage, the protein for preventing freezing. From this we inferred that adaptation requires a match between function and selective origin. The beetle case reveals that a trait may acquire its function indirectly, as a by-product of selection acting on a different property. Our inductive sample consisted entirely of traits whose functions were the direct targets of selection, leading us to mistake this coincidence for a necessary connexion.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of adaptation present a tidy picture. The giraffe's neck, the moth's colouration, the fish's antifreeze proteins — in each instance, the trait confers a fitness advantage, arose through the gradual accumulation of heritable variations under natural selection, and serves the very function for which selection favoured it. From these examples one might confidently generalise: a trait is an adaptation when it enhances fitness, arose through gradual selection, and serves a specific function that explains its selective history. The three conditions appear to capture what biologists mean when they call a trait adaptive.

Yet the neatness of this generalisation may owe more to the neatness of the cases from which it was drawn than to the underlying nature of adaptation itself. Let us imagine a species of beetle that produces a potent toxin, synthesised by a particular enzyme, which renders it unpalatable to predators. The gene encoding this enzyme also has a pleiotropic effect: it produces a bright red pigment in the beetle's exoskeleton. The red colouration was not selected for its own sake; it appeared as a side-effect of the gene that was selected for toxin production. Yet the red colour functions as an aposematic signal, warning would-be predators of the beetle's noxiousness, and it plainly confers a fitness advantage. It serves a recognisable function, and it enhances survival. But it did not arise through the gradual accumulation of variations selected specifically for warning colouration. It appeared, fully formed, as a consequence of selection acting upon a chemically unrelated trait. The second condition of our analysis — that the trait arose through gradual selection for the function it now serves — is not satisfied. We are thus brought to recognise that our original induction was drawn from cases in which selective history and current function happened to align, and that we mistakenly elevated this alignment to the status of a necessary condition. The beetle's pigmentation teaches us that adaptation may arise by routes more circuitous than our paradigmatic examples suggested.

background_cases: A child contracts measles: a virus invades her body, she develops a rash and fever, and her normal physiological functioning is disrupted. We say she has a disease.
A man develops tuberculosis: the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis colonises his lungs, producing cough, weight loss, and progressive impairment. This is a clear case of disease.
A woman is bitten by a tick and contracts Lyme disease: the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi causes joint pain, fatigue, and neurological symptoms.
definition: An organism has a disease if and only if (i) a specific pathogenic agent has invaded the organism, (ii) the agent disrupts the organism's normal physiological functioning, and (iii) the organism exhibits recognisable clinical symptoms.
conditions: a specific pathogenic agent has invaded the organism
the agent disrupts the organism's normal physiological functioning
the organism exhibits recognisable clinical symptoms
counterexample: Suppose that a man has a hereditary condition in which his own cells produce an excess of a particular protein, leading to the progressive degeneration of his nervous system. No pathogenic agent — no virus, no bacterium, no fungus — has invaded his body. The disruption arises from within, from a fault in his own genetic programme. His physiological functioning is disrupted and he exhibits unmistakable clinical symptoms — tremor, muscle wasting, cognitive decline — yet the first condition is not satisfied. It appears, however, that he has a disease in every meaningful sense of the term.
abductive_insight: Our background cases were all infectious diseases caused by identifiable pathogens — a virus, a bacterium, a spirochete. From these we inferred that the presence of a specific pathogenic agent is a necessary condition of disease. The hereditary case reveals that our inductive base was entirely composed of infectious conditions, leading us to mistake a common cause of disease for a necessary condition of it. Disease may originate from within the organism's own biology, without any external invasion, and our generalisation was unduly narrow.
passage:
The cases that most readily come to mind when we think of disease involve the invasion of the body by a foreign organism. The measles virus, the tubercle bacillus, the Lyme spirochete — in each instance, a specific pathogenic agent enters the body, disrupts its normal functioning, and produces recognisable symptoms. From these cases one might naturally infer that disease consists in the conjunction of three conditions: invasion by a pathogenic agent, disruption of normal physiology, and the exhibition of clinical symptoms. The inference appears sound, for the three conditions are present in every case we have considered, and they seem jointly to capture what it is for an organism to be diseased.

Yet the strength of this inference rests upon the breadth of the cases from which it was drawn, and we should examine whether that breadth is sufficient. Let us imagine a person who, in middle age, begins to experience a fine tremor in his hands. Over the ensuing years, the tremor worsens; his muscles waste; his cognitive faculties decline. Investigation reveals that the cause lies not in any invading organism but in his own genetic constitution: a heritable defect causes his cells to produce an excess of a particular protein, which accumulates in his nervous tissue and progressively destroys it. No pathogen has entered his body. The first condition of the proposed analysis — invasion by a specific pathogenic agent — is wholly unsatisfied. Yet the man's physiological functioning is profoundly disrupted, and his clinical symptoms are unmistakable. It appears beyond reasonable dispute that he has a disease. We are thus brought to recognise that our original generalisation was drawn from an unrepresentative sample — one consisting entirely of infectious conditions — and that we mistakenly elevated the presence of a pathogenic agent to the status of a necessary condition. The hereditary case teaches us that disease may arise from the organism's own internal processes, requiring no invasion from without, and that the concept of disease is broader than our initial induction suggested.

background_cases: The gene encoding haemoglobin directs the synthesis of a protein essential for oxygen transport in vertebrate blood. Mutations in this gene produce recognisable disorders such as sickle-cell anaemia.

A gene in maize encodes an enzyme responsible for the synthesis of anthocyanin, the pigment that gives certain kernels their purple colour. The gene's product can be traced from DNA sequence to enzymatic function to visible phenotype.
The gene for lactase persistence in certain human populations encodes an enzyme that enables the digestion of lactose into adulthood, conferring an advantage upon pastoralist populations.
definition: A segment of DNA is a gene if and only if (i) it encodes a protein that performs a specific biological function, (ii) its expression produces a detectable phenotypic effect, and (iii) mutations in the segment produce recognisable alterations in the phenotype.
conditions: it encodes a protein that performs a specific biological function
its expression produces a detectable phenotypic effect
mutations in the segment produce recognisable alterations in the phenotype
counterexample: Suppose that a segment of DNA does not encode a protein at all but is transcribed into a small RNA molecule that regulates the expression of other genes by binding to their messenger RNA and preventing translation. This regulatory RNA performs a critical biological function — the organism's development is severely disrupted when the segment is deleted — yet the segment does not encode a protein. The first condition is unsatisfied, and yet molecular biologists would classify the segment as a gene, for it is transcribed, it performs a function, and its disruption has profound consequences.
abductive_insight: Our background cases were all protein-coding genes with clear phenotypic effects, and from these we generalised that encoding a protein is a necessary condition of gene status. The regulatory RNA case reveals that our inductive sample was drawn from a single functional category — protein-coding sequences — and that we mistakenly elevated this category to the definition of the concept. The concept of a gene has broadened as molecular biology has discovered functional DNA segments that operate through mechanisms other than protein synthesis, and our original generalisation was too narrow to accommodate this diversity.
passage:
The cases from which our ordinary concept of a gene is most readily drawn are all protein-coding sequences with visible phenotypic effects. The haemoglobin gene encodes a protein vital for oxygen transport; the anthocyanin gene encodes an enzyme that produces pigment; the lactase gene encodes an enzyme that digests a sugar. In each instance, the segment of DNA directs the synthesis of a protein with a specific biological function, the expression of the gene produces a detectable effect upon the organism's phenotype, and mutations in the segment yield recognisable phenotypic alterations. From these cases, one might confidently infer that a gene is a DNA segment that encodes a functional protein, produces a phenotypic effect, and is subject to phenotype-altering mutations.

Yet we should consider whether the uniformity of these cases has narrowed our generalisation unduly. Let us imagine a segment of DNA that does not encode any protein. Instead, it is transcribed into a small RNA molecule — a few dozen nucleotides in length — that binds to the messenger RNA of other genes and prevents their translation into protein. This regulatory RNA performs a function of the first importance: when the DNA segment is experimentally deleted, the organism's development goes gravely awry, producing severe abnormalities. The segment is transcribed, it performs a biological function, and its disruption has dramatic consequences. Yet it does not encode a protein. The first condition of our proposed analysis is not satisfied. Nevertheless, molecular biologists would classify this segment as a gene without hesitation, for it is a heritable, functional unit of the genome. We are thus brought to recognise that our original induction was drawn exclusively from protein-coding sequences and that we mistakenly treated protein-coding as a necessary feature of all genes. The regulatory RNA case teaches us that the concept of a gene must be capacious enough to include functional elements that operate through mechanisms other than the synthesis of protein.
